summarization.a: Schematic of the hot-wall reactor showing five TiN sample positions distributed over 12.7 cm, with reactant flow from left to right toward the pump.
summarization.b: Film thickness versus sample location before and after 200 TiN ALE cycles using O₃ and HF at 250°C. The initial TiN thickness is uniform at approximately 75 Å across all positions. After ALE, positions 2 through 5 show consistent etching with an etch rate of 0.19 Å/cycle, while position 1 (nearest to reactant entry) shows slightly higher etching at 0.27 Å/cycle.

a.question: How does the etch rate vary with sample position at 250°C?
a.answer: At 250°C, the TiN ALE process shows good uniformity across most of the reactor. Positions 2 through 5 all exhibit a consistent etch rate of approximately 0.19 Å/cycle. However, position 1, closest to the reactant entry, shows a slightly higher etch rate of 0.27 Å/cycle. This enhanced etching near the inlet likely results from higher local reactant concentrations before any depletion occurs along the flow path.

a.question: What is the purpose of placing multiple samples at different reactor positions?
a.answer: To assess the spatial uniformity of the TiN ALE process and detect any effects from reactant depletion or decomposition along the reactor.

a.question: Is the TiN ALE process uniform enough for practical applications at 250°C?
a.answer: The results suggest reasonably good uniformity at 250°C. Four out of five positions show identical etch rates, with only the position nearest the reactant inlet displaying elevated etching. For semiconductor manufacturing applications where precise thickness control is critical, this level of uniformity would be acceptable for most of the reactor volume, though the inlet region may require optimization or sample positioning adjustments.

a.question: What was the initial TiN film thickness used in this uniformity study?
a.answer: Approximately 75 Å, uniform across all five sample positions.
